course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the basic principles of taxation law in Australia, with particular emphasis on the topics required by professional accounting bodies for accreditation purposes.

requirements.restrictions: Not for students enrolled in LLB(Hons)(#64), LLB(Hons)(#48), LLB, LLB (Grad Entry), LLB(Hons) duals and LLB dual programs
requirements.incompatible: LAWS5144 or LAWS7012 or LAWS7144 or LAWS7344
requirements.assumed_background: You are assumed to have studied and passed LAWS1100 Business Law (or its equivalent) and to have retained from that course an understanding of the Australian legal system and the ability to apply legal knowledge and legal reasoning in the analysis and resolution of legal problems.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Due to the Labour Day public holiday, the Monday timetable of Week 9 will be taught on the Tuesday of Week 9. Refer to Section 4 of the ECP for the detailed learning activities.

Due to public holidays, there will be no tutorial classes during week 8 and 9.

Normal learning activities (including lectures and tutorials, as set out in Section 4 of the ECP) will continue during the week in which the Mid Semester Exam will be written.

Lectures will be recorded and posted to the Learning Resources folder in the course Blackboard site.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims:

To equip students with sufficient knowledge in Australian income tax law to enable them to deal with clients, business persons and legal advisers in an informed and reasoned manner.

To demonstrate the development of legal principles through case law and statutory provisions.

To equip students with the skill and technique to calculate taxable income, tax payable, Fringe Benefit Tax payable and Goods and Services Tax payable or refundable.

To instil an attitude of professionalism and an awareness of ethical standards with regard to:

presentation of written work;

dealings with University staff;

interaction with peers; and

interaction with members of the professions.

To enhance verbal communication skills by encouraging students to participate in discussion and debate of legal issues.

To inspire students to approach the study of law with a keen and inquiring mind, and to maintain life-long interest in legal issues.

learning_outcomes.description: Prove a knowledge of the regulatory framework of the taxation system in Australia.
Demonstrate an understanding of the income tax equation and the elements which are the basis of it and be able to apply those elements to the case of a taxpayer.

Interpret provisions of the relevant Income Tax Assessment Act and other relevant legislation and apply them by preparing a concise written argument sufficient to form the basis of sound advice to a taxpayer and/or by calculating the financial implications of a particular transaction.
Critically evaluate case law as well as Rulings & Determinations issued by the Commissioner of Taxation.
Solve legal problems/issues relating to assessable income, deductions, capital gains tax, tax payable and tax offsets, Fringe Benefit Tax and the Goods and Services Tax.
Demonstrate the ability to research broad issues in the area of income tax law.
Discuss legal issues raised relating to taxation law in a confident and informed manner.

assessment_summary.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_summary.title: Mid Semester Exam
assessment_summary.due_date: Exam will be scheduled by central examinations for a Saturday time.

assessment_details.title: Mid Semester Exam
assessment_details.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_details.due_date: Exam will be scheduled by central examinations for a Saturday time.
assessment_details.format: Short answer, Short essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The Mid Semester Exam will contain short theory questions, short essay questions, practical problems and problem solving questions. Therefore, the format of the Mid Semester Exam is different to that of previous semesters.

The Mid Semester Exam will be based on material covered in Topic 1, Topic 2, Topic 3 and the first lecture of Topic 4 (which will be clearly set out in the Lecture Slides).

Students are not required to follow the ILAC-approach (Identify the issue – State the law – Apply the law to the facts – Conclude) when answering the questions.

The Mid Semester Exam is an Open Book exam. Bi-lingual dictionaries are permitted, but no electronic devices and watches are allowed in the exam venues.

Students must bring a University approved calculator to the Mid Semester Exam.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Work and Participation
assessment_details.task_description:
There are ten (10) tutorials for this course. Attendance at all tutorials is strongly recommended. Each tutorial will have a set of tutorial tasks that are to be completed in writing and printed out before attending the relevant tutorial.

Students' answers do not have to be correct but must contain sufficient material to demonstrate a professional attempt at the prescribed tasks to be considered for marks.

A student who presents a typed answer sheet to all tutorial tasks for at least seven (7) of the ten (10) tutorials at the commencement of that tutorial and who participates in the relevant tutorial will be awarded 1.5 marks per tutorial class session, with a maximum of ten (10) marks that can be awarded to tutorial work and participation . Students are to retain the copy of their presented tutorial work.

A maximum of 10 marks for the whole semester will be awarded for this form of assessment.

Answers to tutorial tasks will not be posted to Blackboard . Therefore, it is important that students prepare sufficiently detailed answers to tutorial tasks so that they are able to add to and/or amend answers during tutorial classes.

Students who cannot attend tutorials due to illness may discuss their answers with teaching staff during consultation times.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

Submission:

Presented to tutor at commencement of relevant tutorial.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description:
The Final Exam will cover material from all topics. The questions will be problem style questions that include discussion and calculation type questions.

A detailed Final Exam guideline will be posted to the course Blackboard site in week 12.

Students are not required to follow the ILAC-approach (Identify the issue - State the law - Apply the law to the facts - Conclude) when answering questions.

The Final Exam will be an Open Book exam. Bi-lingual dictionaries are permitted, but no electronic devices and watches will be allowed.

Students will need a University approved calculator .

learning_resources.required_resources:
LAWS3101 Course Website (Blackboard).

The LAWS3101 Blackboard site contains most of the material required for this course.

Students must check the course Blackboard site at least twice weekly.

Pay careful attention to the readings for each topic.

The Learning Resources folder contains the lecture recordings.

The Learning Activities folder is in topic order and contains the study notes, case studies, lecture slides and prescribed reference material. The material for each topic will be made available a week in advance of each lecture.

A separate folder contains all of the Tutorial Tasks.

Principles of Taxation Law (2016). Thomson Reuters. Kerrie Sadiq et al.

As income tax law changes constantly, you should ensure that you obtain the 2016 edition of this texbook.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
Students may find resources such as the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website, incorporating the ATO Legal Database helpful.

There is a useful list of internet resources in Principles of Taxation Law at [2.480].

Fundamental Tax Legislation (2016). Thomson Reuters. Pinto, Kendall, Sadiq.

This book contains income tax related legislation. Students who aim for a grade of 6 or 7 in this course will often refer to the legislation.

The legislation is also available online:

· Australian Legal Information Institute, www.austlii.edu.au

· ATO, www.ato.gov.au

Australian Taxation Law Cases (2016). Thomson Reuters. Krever.

Students who wish to engage further with the cases referenced in Principles of Taxation Law will find this textbook useful.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS3101 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (3rd edition) .

The Law Library has produced a ‘How-to Guide’ to assist students to follow the AGLC3 citation style.

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Blackboard Website

Course Blackboard websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Blackboard websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Blackboard websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Blackboard is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the Law School Webmaster by email – webmaster@law.uq.edu.au .

AskIT , available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://www.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
The course Blackboard site will contain all necessary resources and information not included in the prescribed texts.

learning_activities.topic: Mon - Tue Week 1 : Topic 1: The Taxation System in Australia. This topic introduces students to Income Tax, Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefit Tax. The topic then focuses on the Income Tax equation and a range of income-based taxes that are payable. The topic concludes with basic principles around tax avoidance and how the tax system in Australia is administered. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 1); PoTL (Chapters 1, 2, 3, 15, 19, 23 & 24)

Mon - Tue Week 2 : Topic 2: Residence, source and tax accounting. This topic examines the key concepts of residence and source and how these impact income tax payable. It further examines when income is derived and expenses incurred. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL (Chapters 4 & 16)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 1 : Material from Topic 1 Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 1); PoTL (Readings for Topic 1)

Mon - Tue Week 3 : Topic 3: Income | Lecture 1. This topic examines the concept of income from employment, property and carrying on a business. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL (Chapters 5, 6, 8, 9, 10 & 21)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 2 : Material from Topic 2 Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL (Readings for Topic 2)

Mon - Tue Week 4 : Topic 3: Income | Lecture 2. This topic examines the concept of income from employment, property and carrying on a business. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL (Chapter 5, 6, 8, 9, 10 & 21)

Mon - Fri Tutorial 3 : Material from Topic 2 and Topic 3 | Lecture 1 Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 2 and Topic 3); PoTL (Readings for Topic 2 and Topic 3)
Mon - Sun MID SEMESTER BREAK : NO CLASSES

Mon - Tue Week 5 : Topic 4: Deductions | Lecture 1. This topic examines a wide range of deductions that include immediate deductions and capital allowances. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL (Chapter 12, 13, 14, 16 & 18)
Mon - Sun Tutorial 4 : Material from Topic 3 Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL (Readings for Topic 3)

Mon - Tue Week 6 : Topic 4: Deductions | Lecture 2. This topic examines a wide range of deductions that include immediate deductions and capital allowances. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL (Chapter 12, 13, 14, 16 & 18)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 5 : Material from Topic 4 Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL (Readings for Topic 4)

Mon - Tue Week 7 : Topic 5: Trading stock and a revision question related to the carrying on of a business. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 5 and revision question); PoTL (Chapter 17)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 6 : Material from Topic 4 Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4 ); PoTL (Readings for Topic 4)

Tue - Tue Week 8 - No class on Monday : Topic 6: Capital Gains Tax | Lecture 1 for Tuesday class sign-on students. This topic introduces students to CGT and the determination of the net capital gain. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 6); PoTL (Chapter 11)
Mon - Sun No Tutorials : No tutorials scheduled during this week.

Mon - Tue Week 9 Class for Monday sign-on students : Topic 6 Capital Gains Tax | Lecture 1. This class is only for Monday class sign-on and the lecture will take place on the Tuesday, but following the Monday time table. This lecture is therefore a repeat of the lecture held in the previous week for Tuesday sign-on students. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 6); PoTL (Chapters 11)
Mon - Sun No Tutorials : No tutorials scheduled during this week.

Mon - Tue Week 10 : Topic 6: Capital gains tax | Lecture 2. This topic introduces students to CGT and the determination of the net capital gain. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 6); PoTL (Chapter 11)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 7 : Material from Topic 5 and Topic 6 Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 5 and Topic 6); PoTL (Readings for Topic 5 and Topic 6)

Mon - Tue Week 11 : Topic 7: GST and Topic 8: FBT. The lecture will focus on GST and an introduction to FBT. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 7 and Topic 8); PoTL (Chapter 7 & 25)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 8 : Material from Topic 4 and Topic 6 Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4 and Topic 6); PoTL (Readings for Topic 4 and Topic 6)
Mon - Tue Week 12 : Topic 8: Fringe Benefits Tax (FBT). Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 8); PoTL (Chapter 7)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 9 : Material from Topic 7 Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 7); PoTL (Readings for Topic 7)

Mon - Tue Revision Lecture : Review of the key concepts discussed during lectures by using an extended revision question and a discussion of the details of the Final Exam format. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Revision Lecture)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University’s assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment

Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

There are several feedback opportunities for students in this course:

Tutorial classes provide students with small-group feedback about their answers to particular questions.

The Mid Semester Exam's detailed answers will be posted to the course Blackboard site to provide feedback related to the law and appropriate exam technique.

In-class quizzes at the end of some lecture periods will recap on students' understanding of topics.

Feedback during consultation hours.

Students are encouraged to email their questions to the course coordinator during the course of the semester. This is a unique feature of the feedback in this course.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( https://ssl.law.uq.edu.au/teaching-handbook/TL14-Feedback-on-final-examinations.pdf ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 6 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations and may take the format of an oral examination. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to myAdvisor for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
The overall aim of this introductory course in income tax law is to provide students with comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of Income Tax, Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefit Tax. This course takes a practical approach to income tax law.

The course examines principles of Income Tax law, including an analysis of the concepts of income, assessable income, allowable deductions and capital gains, and the calculation of tax payable. The course also examines Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefit Tax and how these two taxes interact with Income Tax. It is not the objective of this course to study in detail all of the specific and technical provisions in the legislation which govern matters which affects the actual preparation of income tax returns and business activity statements.

Janine McMaster-Kirkwood Prize in Income Tax Law : The student with the best performance in their first attempt at LAWS3101 will be recommended for this prize.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Changes to the course material, assessments, text books and the tutorial program have been implemented to take into account prior year feedback.

Specifically, the sequence in which topics will be studied, compared to previous semesters, has been changed. Therefore, the format of the Mid Semester Exam will change to reflect a greater emphasis on calculations.

Extracts from applicable legislation will be included in the topic study guides and will form an integral part of the work which is examinable.

Two revision lectures have been included in the semester program.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .

C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where, within an analytical framework offered by the lecturer, students and the lecturer can:

Explore concepts of law relevant to the topic,

Explore how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts learned,

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration.

In a negative sense, lectures are not for:

Delivering an exclusive and exhaustive account of the material that the student needs to learn,

Reading material that students can read for themselves.

The Function of Tutorials

Tutorials offer students an interactive forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures and to engage with the tutorial staff and other students in exploring those issues. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity to develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

As an interactive learning forum, the tutorial is dependent upon all students having prepared the material to be considered and being prepared to engage in debate.

Tutorials are not an opportunity to take down answers.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85% +
late_submission:
Where a non-examination item of assessment (eg essays, assignments etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day that the assessment is overdue.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 4pm on Monday, assessment submitted after 4pm Monday but before 4pm on Tuesday will receive a 10% penalty.

Assessment submitted more than 5 days after the due date and time will receive zero marks.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 4pm on the 10th, any work submitted after 4pm on the 15th will receive zero marks.

If the 5th day following the due date is a Saturday or a Sunday, assessment will be accepted until 9am on the following Monday .

Example: A piece of assessment is due at 4pm on Monday the 9th. In the normal course of events, to receive any marks, a student must submit their assessment by 4pm on Saturday the 14th (the 5th day following the due date) at the latest. As the 5th day is a Saturday, the student may submit anytime up to 9am on Monday the 16th.

No extension granted may exceed 14 days.

For full details, students should refer to the Guidelines for Late Submission of Progressive Assessment . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for assessment will only be considered in respect of a certified medical condition . Applications for extension shall be made by emailing the Application for Extension of Assessment Due Date form (and supporting documentation) to extensions@law.uq.edu.au by the due date for assessment unless the exceptional circumstance or medical condition is such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have applied prior to the due date.

Unless instructed otherwise by the Law School, students who are granted an extension beyond the due date for assessment items must submit their assessment to the Law School Office, NOT the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre.
other_assessment_information: The Mid Semester Exam and Final Exam are Open Book exams. Students may bring any material (except on electronic devices) with them into the exam venues. But, as there is limited time available to answer questions under exam conditions, it is not practical to look up all answers. It is therefore important that students have an effective plan for the Open Book exams. One approach would be to prepare a summary of the most important points from the legislation in alphabetical order and to expand and amend this summary as the semester progresses. Due to space constraints in exam venues, it would probably not be practical to bring all of the course material to the exam venues.
